comments.text: hahaha mate, joins the club! 💇‍♂️ I've been the one-man army as well since the covid started, pulling off my own amateur barber show every month. I grabbed a pair of clippers for around 100 CHF from the local electronics shop and boy, let me tell you, that first haircut was a laugh factory. Had a half-decent mullet going for a short while 🤣

thankfully with time I've actually gotten pretty decent. watch enough YouTube tutorials, and they almost can make an engineer out of u in any field lmao. plus, saves me from the eye watering Zürich barbershop prices. gotta love swiss living amirite? having high income definitely doesn't mean I'm looking to throw it away 🙄 plus more $$$ for my reddit gold subscription and other online goodies 😌
anyone else tried cutting their own hair and have some funny/ disastrous stories to share? 😂 or any tips for a better self-haircut are welcome too. gotta love the WaldGänger lifestyle at times. ✌️💈🇨🇭

income.inference: The author mentions living in Switzerland, specifically Zürich, which is known for its high cost of living and high average incomes. The mention of "having high income" suggests that the author considers themselves to be earning well. They also talk about saving money by not going to expensive barbershops and mention having extra money for online subscriptions like Reddit Gold, which implies discretionary spending. The use of CHF (Swiss Franc) indicates they are earning in a strong currency. Additionally, the casual mention of engineering ("watch enough YouTube tutorials, and they almost can make an engineer out of u") could imply a technical background, which often correlates with higher income levels. However, the author's willingness to save money by cutting their own hair and the focus on not wanting to waste money could suggest a frugal mindset rather than extremely high earnings.

comments.text: lol, '98 graduation? “you kids and your Spice Girls.” just teasing ya! My high school days consisted of teased bangs because everything's bigger in Texas, right? And... the horror... neon windbreakers. Yup, our version of 'cool'. Good grief, why did we think shoulder pads were the way to go? Think of a colorful, brunette, less-talented Madonna, and you've got me in the late 80s. Total cringe-fest. But yeah, to be fair to young me, it was the era, but still... what were we thinking? haha!

comments.text: oh man, i can relate an unfortunate hair experience. i've had mine cut into what can only be described as 'helmet hair' once. it took a good month to grow back into a more, shall we say, 'human' look. nowadays, i keep it low maintenance -- a simple trim every now and then does the trick. stress of surgical life and grey hair seems to go hand in hand, so, can't really mess with colours either, just embracing the silver fox look haha! lesson learned anyway: sometimes simpler is better.

comments.text: My Masters in Computer Science was nothing short of an adrenaline filled, caffeine powered roller coaster ride. I mean every exam felt like it could turn things around. And yep, sleep? what is that again? lol. Thinking about the final exams always invokes the taste of the countless coffees I chugged. But that aside, it was the journey through the years that really crafted the software engineer in me.

The only thing is, the exams themselves didn't feel like introducing grand new paradigms. Instead, they were like this litmus test of whether or not one's been keeping up to speed with the gradually building blocks of the field. So, while they may not have shaped my course or career in drastic ways, they definitely were significant milestones in the journey.

And although I might not have realized it then, there's definitely a fair bit of what I learnt that I use now in my job here in Zurich. Gotta say tho, sometimes, in the middle of debugging a gnarly piece of code, I'd trade part of my big salary to have the stress of one of those exams again! haha. :P

education.inference: The author explicitly mentions having a "Masters in Computer Science," which indicates that they have completed a graduate-level degree. The mention of exams and the gradual building blocks of the field implies a structured educational experience typical of formal education. Additionally, the author refers to their job in Zurich, which could imply that they are working in a professional capacity that typically requires a high level of education. comments.text: You know, I've often found myself reflecting on that too. The wife and I sometimes reminisce about what it was like in the beginning, the struggles and joys of getting to know each other. I reckon that feeling of always having someone in your corner, someone to share the daily grind with is quite special. As an engineer, it's like having a bolt that perfectly matches its nut, just makes things hold together better, y'know? Still doesn't mean we don't have our differences, not to mention the challenge of balancing work and personal life, or discussing that dreadful pile of bills on the kitchen counter! 😂 But I guess that's part and parcel of companionship. Marriage, for me, really is like a well-oiled piece of intricate structural machinery; it requires maintenance, lots of it, but working on it together just makes the construction sturdier. Totally worth it, in my humble opinion!

married.inference: The author explicitly mentions a spouse by referring to "The wife and I" which indicates a marital relationship. The use of past tense in "reminisce" suggests a shared history. The author also talks about shared responsibilities and challenges such as balancing work and personal life, and discussing bills, which are common aspects of a domestic partnership, typically in marriage. The engineering analogy of a bolt and nut, along with the mention of marriage being like "a well-oiled piece of intricate structural machinery," further reinforces the notion of a committed, long-term relationship. The language used indicates a deep familiarity and ongoing partnership that is characteristic of marriage.
